content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
February 24,
1993
Kenneth A.
Wilson
Meyers,
Nave,
Riback
& Silver
50 old Courthouse Square,
Suite
603
Santa Rosa,
CA
95404
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93-035
Dear Mr.
Wilson:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Healdsburg Councilmember
Tom Chambers,
concerning his
duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act
(the ‘Act”).
2
-/
QUESTION
Is Councilmember Chambers prohibited from participating in
decisions concerning the development,
or land use alternatives,
on
property located within 1,200 feet of his real property interests?
CONCLUSION
Councilmember Chambers may not participate in decisions
concerning the development or use of property 1,200 feet from his
property interests if the decision will have a material financial
effect
on his real property interests.
FACTS
Councilmember Chambers,
together with his wife,
owns
a
residence within the city’s redevelopment project area.
He and
his wife also
own a one—half interest in an adjoining residence
from which they derive income.
Both properties are beyond 300
feet,
but within 1,200 feet,
from the downtown site referred to as
the
“West Plaza.”
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000
.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No.
A—93—035
Page Two
The West Plaza is a vacant lot owned by the city.
There have
been attempts in the past to develop the property as a hotel with
shops as part of the city’s redevelopment program.
There
presently is not a specific proposed development for the West
Plaza site,
but it is contemplated that the city may consider
other uses for the site,
such as a city park of other public use.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable
2
! that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official,
or on a member of the official’s immediate
family,
or on:
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has
a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(Section 87103(b) .)
We are assuming that the value of Councilmember Chamber’s
property meets the $1,000 threshold.
Thus,
he would be prohibited
from participating in decisions which would have a material
financial effect on his real property interests.
To determine if a decision will have a material effect,
we
look at Commission Regulation 18702.3 which provides that the
effect of a decision is material if the real property in which the
official has an interest is located outside a radius of
300 feet.
and any part of the real property is located within a radius of
2,500 feet of the boundaries of the property which is the subject
of the decision and the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of $10,000 or more on the fair market
value of the property;
or will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per
12 month period.
(Regulation
18702.3(a) (3).)
2/
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)


Advice File No.
A-93-035
Page Three
You indicated that Councilmember Chamber’s property is
approximately 1,200 feet from the site referred to as
“West
Plaza.”
Accordingly,
he would not be permitted to participate in
any decisions,
including alternative use decisions, if the
decision could increase or decrease the value of his property by
$10,000;
or affect the rental value of his property by $1,000 or
more per
12. month period.
In addition,
in some cases,
decisions may be too interrelated
to be considered separately.
If the resolution of one decision
will effectively determine the result of the other decision,
the
councilmeinber must disqualify himself as to both.
For example, if
a decision is to place a hotel on one of two locations,
the
decision to place the hotel at one location necessarily decides
whether the hotel is to be placed at the alternate location.
Disqualification would still be required if the councilmember
owned the alternate location.
We have previously advised that a public official is required
to make a reasonable good faith effort to determine the financial
effects of a decision on his interests in real property.
(McLaughlin Advice Letter,
No.
1-91-141.)
Please note that
pursuant to subdivision
(d)
of Regulation 18702.3,
factors which
must be considered in determining whether a governmental decision
will have a material financial effect on an official’s real
property include,
but are not limited to:
(1)
The proximity of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the magnitude of the
proposed project or change in use in relationship to the
property in which the official has an interest;
(2)
Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will affect the development potential or income
producing potential of the property;
(3)
In addition to the foregoing,
in the case of
residential property,
whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will result in a change to
the character of the neighborhood including, but not
limited to,
effects on traffic,
view, privacy,
intensity
of use,
noise levels, air emissions,
or similar traits
of the neighborhood.
(Regulation 18702.3(d).)
Accordingly,
once Councilmember Chambers makes a reasonable
good faith estimate to determine the material financial effect on
the value of his real property,
and that effect will be material,


Advice File No.
A—93—035
Page Four
as described above,
Councilmember Chambers would be prohibited
from making or participating in the making of decisions concerning
the site known as
“West Plaza.”
I trust this answers your question.
If we can be of further
assistance, please feel free to contact the Legal Division at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General Counsel
B
:
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
JM/JET/ jt

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is Councilmember Chambers prohibited from participating in
decisions concerning the development,
or land use alternatives,
on
property located within 1,200 feet of his real property interests?

CONCLUSION: Councilmember Chambers may not participate in decisions
concerning the development or use of property 1,200 feet from his
property interests if the decision will have a material financial
effect
on his real property interests.
